title: Preservation Competence & System Connections
pillar_name: Regenerative Food Systems, Animal Stewardship & Land Care

required.title: [Book] Ball Complete Book of Home Preserving
required.author: edited by Judi Kingry and Lauren Devine

suggested.title: [Book] Preserving Food Without Freezing or Canning
suggested.author: the Gardeners and Farmers of Terre Vivante

supplementary.title: [Reference] USDA Complete Guide to Home Canning (free online)
[Reference] National Center for Home Food Preservation (nchfp.uga.edu)

competence_markers.description: The student has safely completed water bath canning with proper technique and all seals successful
The student has safely completed pressure canning with proper technique and understanding of safety requirements
The student produces fermented vegetables at household-supply scale
The student can dehydrate food to proper dryness standards and store for extended shelf life
The student plans garden production with preservation quantities in mind
The student can draw a system flow diagram showing connections between enterprises on their property
The student maintains a preserved food inventory and tracks household use across seasons

content.why_this_matters:
You have dried herbs, frozen vegetables, and made sauerkraut. Now you develop full competence in multiple preservation methods — the skills that turn a seasonal harvest into a year-round food supply. This is not a hobby or an interesting skill. This is the technology that determines whether your garden, orchard, and animals actually feed you through winter or just produce a summer surplus that rots because you could not process it fast enough.

The preservation problem is real and ancient. Every productive land system produces most of its food in a few intense weeks: tomatoes ripen all at once in August, the apple harvest comes in October, the meat chickens are ready to process in one weekend. If you cannot preserve that abundance, you feast for a week and then have nothing. Preservation converts peaks of abundance into a steady supply — and mastering it is what makes the difference between a productive hobby and genuine food sovereignty.

This packet also asks you to start thinking explicitly about your land system as a designed whole. Not just "the garden" and "the chickens" and "the orchard" operating in parallel — but all of them connected, with the outputs of each enterprise feeding the inputs of another. This is the beginning of the integrated system design that 2.5.8-C will bring to full expression.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Water bath canning** — safely preserving high-acid foods for shelf-stable storage
2. **Pressure canning** — safely preserving low-acid foods (the most critical safety skill in home preservation)
3. **Lacto-fermentation at scale** — producing meaningful quantities of fermented vegetables
4. **Dehydration** — systematic drying of vegetables, fruits, herbs, and meat
5. **Cold storage management** — optimizing storage conditions for different crops
6. **Harvest planning** — growing with preservation in mind, calculating quantities, timing harvests
7. **System mapping** — explicitly diagramming nutrient and energy flows between enterprises
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Water Bath Canning — Preserving High-Acid Foods

Water bath canning preserves food by heating sealed jars in boiling water long enough to kill spoilage organisms. It works ONLY for high-acid foods — foods with a pH below 4.6.

**Foods safe for water bath canning:**
- Fruits and fruit products (jams, jellies, fruit butter, pie filling)
- Pickles and relishes (acid from vinegar provides the safety margin)
- Tomatoes WITH added acid (2 tablespoons lemon juice or 1/2 teaspoon citric acid per quart — tomatoes alone are borderline pH and must have acid added)
- Salsa made from USDA-tested recipes only

**Why pH matters:** Below pH 4.6, Clostridium botulinum — the bacterium that produces botulinum toxin — cannot grow. Botulinum toxin is one of the most potent toxins known. A single jar of improperly canned low-acid food can kill. This is why the pH boundary is non-negotiable and why low-acid foods require pressure canning (Part 2).

**The process:**
1. Sterilize jars in boiling water for 10 minutes (or use jars directly from a hot dishwasher cycle)
2. Prepare the food according to a TESTED recipe — not your grandmother's recipe, not a blog post, not an improvisation. Tested recipes have been validated for pH and processing time. Use them exactly.
3. Fill hot jars, leaving the specified headspace (the air gap between food and jar rim — usually 1/4 inch for jams, 1/2 inch for fruits and pickles)
4. Remove air bubbles by running a non-metallic utensil around the inside of the jar
5. Wipe the jar rim clean — any food residue on the rim prevents a proper seal
6. Apply a new lid and tighten the band fingertip-tight (snug, not forced)
7. Lower jars into the boiling water bath. Water must cover jar lids by at least 1 inch.
8. Process for the time specified in the recipe (adjusted for your altitude — water boils at lower temperatures at elevation, requiring longer processing times)
9. Remove jars and let cool undisturbed for 12-24 hours
10. Check seals: press the center of each lid. If it does not flex, the seal is good. If it pops, the seal failed — refrigerate and use within a week, or reprocess with a new lid.

### Part 2: Pressure Canning — The Safety-Critical Skill

Low-acid foods — vegetables, meat, poultry, fish, soups, and beans — have a pH above 4.6. At this pH, C. botulinum can grow and produce toxin. Boiling water (212 degrees Fahrenheit at sea level) is NOT hot enough to kill botulinum spores. Only the higher temperatures achievable in a pressure canner (240 degrees Fahrenheit at 10 PSI) kill the spores.

**This is not optional.** If you can low-acid food in a water bath, you are creating conditions for botulism — a potentially fatal poisoning with no visible, smell, or taste indicators. The food looks, smells, and tastes fine. You eat it. You may die.

**The process:**
1. Follow a USDA-tested recipe. No exceptions.
2. Fill jars as directed — headspace, bubble removal, clean rims, new lids
3. Place jars on the rack in the pressure canner with 2-3 inches of hot water
4. Lock the lid according to the canner manufacturer's instructions
5. Heat until steam vents steadily from the vent pipe (vent for 10 minutes to exhaust air from the canner)
6. Close the vent (place the weight or close the petcock)
7. Monitor pressure: maintain the pressure specified in the recipe (usually 10 PSI at sea level, adjusted upward for altitude) for the full processing time
8. Turn off heat. Let pressure return to zero NATURALLY — do not release pressure manually
9. Wait 10 minutes after pressure reaches zero. Remove the lid (tilting it away from you — the steam is scalding). Remove jars.
10. Cool undisturbed for 12-24 hours. Check seals.

**Safety rules that are absolute:**
- NEVER process low-acid food in a water bath
- NEVER use a pressure cooker as a pressure canner (they are not designed for it and may not maintain pressure consistently)
- ALWAYS use tested recipes — do not adjust ingredient proportions (changing the ratio of vegetables to liquid can change the pH and processing requirements)
- ALWAYS adjust processing time for altitude
- If a jar seal fails, refrigerate immediately and use within a week

### Part 3: Lacto-Fermentation at Scale

At Stretching Branches, you made a jar of sauerkraut. Now you produce fermented vegetables in meaningful quantities — enough to contribute to household food supply through winter.

**Scaling up:**
- **Sauerkraut:** A 5-gallon crock can ferment 25 pounds of cabbage — enough to fill 10-12 quart jars, providing a quart per week for nearly three months.
- **Kimchi:** The Korean fermented vegetable tradition. More complex than sauerkraut — typically includes napa cabbage, radish, ginger, garlic, and chile pepper. Follow a traditional recipe for your first batch.
- **Fermented pickles:** Cucumbers fermented in salt brine (not vinegar pickles, which are canned, not fermented). Traditional dill pickles — whole cucumbers in a brine with dill, garlic, and spices.
- **Fermented hot sauce:** Peppers blended with salt, fermented for 1-4 weeks, then blended with vinegar. Produces a hot sauce with complexity that bottled hot sauce cannot match.

**Troubleshooting:**
- **Kahm yeast** (a white film on the surface): Harmless. Skim it off and continue. It is unattractive but does not affect safety or flavor significantly.
- **Mold** (fuzzy, colored growth): Harmful. Discard the batch. Mold grows when the vegetables are not fully submerged — always keep food below the brine level.
- **Too salty / not salty enough:** The standard ratio is 2-3% salt by weight (roughly 1 tablespoon per pound of vegetables). Too little salt allows spoilage organisms to compete. Too much produces an unpleasantly salty product that ferments slowly.
- **Soft or mushy texture:** Usually caused by too-warm fermentation temperature. Ideal range is 65-75 degrees Fahrenheit. Above 80 degrees, fermentation is fast but produces soft results.

### Part 4: Dehydration

A dehydrator allows you to dry large quantities of food efficiently and consistently.

**Temperature guidelines:**
- Herbs: 95-105 degrees Fahrenheit (low heat preserves volatile oils)
- Fruits: 130-140 degrees Fahrenheit
- Vegetables: 125-135 degrees Fahrenheit
- Jerky (meat): 155 degrees Fahrenheit minimum (food safety requirement for meat)

**Storage:** Dried food lasts longest when stored in airtight containers with oxygen absorbers, in a cool, dark place. Properly dried and stored food can last 6-12 months (fruits and vegetables) or several years (herbs).

**What to dehydrate:**
- Tomatoes (sun-dried tomato equivalent — for sauces, salads, snacking)
- Peppers (dried peppers for spice blends and cooking)
- Apples (apple chips — one of the best dried-fruit products)
- Herbs (all culinary herbs dry beautifully and maintain potency for months)
- Jerky (seasoned, sliced meat dried to shelf-stable condition)
- Fruit leather (pureed fruit spread thin and dried — homemade fruit roll-ups)

### Part 5: Cold Storage Management

Different crops have different storage requirements:

| Crop | Temperature | Humidity | Duration |
|------|------------|----------|----------|
| Potatoes | 38-45°F | 90-95% | 4-6 months |
| Carrots | 32-35°F | 95-100% | 4-6 months |
| Beets | 32-35°F | 95-100% | 3-5 months |
| Winter squash | 50-55°F | 50-70% | 3-6 months |
| Onions | 32-40°F | 65-70% | 4-8 months |
| Garlic | 32-40°F | 60-70% | 6-8 months |
| Apples | 32-38°F | 90-95% | 2-6 months |
| Cabbage | 32-35°F | 95-100% | 3-4 months |

The challenge: these conditions conflict. Potatoes want cool and moist; squash wants warmer and dry; onions want cool and dry. Managing multiple crops in cold storage requires either multiple storage spaces at different conditions or strategic placement within a single space (higher shelves are warmer; lower are cooler; near a ventilation source is drier).

### Part 6: Harvest Planning — Growing with Preservation in Mind

Production planning starts in the garden:
- How many jars of tomato sauce does your family use per week? Multiply by 52. That is your annual need.
- How many pounds of tomatoes produce one quart of sauce? Roughly 3 pounds.
- How many tomato plants do you need to produce that quantity? A productive tomato plant yields 10-20 pounds.
- Work backward from consumption to production. This is the math that connects the garden plan to the pantry inventory.

Apply the same logic to every crop you preserve: frozen green beans, canned pickles, fermented sauerkraut, dried herbs. A preservation plan that is matched to production is efficient. A preservation plan that is made up during harvest is chaotic.

### Part 7: Mapping System Connections

Draw a diagram of your land system showing every enterprise (garden, orchard, animals, compost, water system) and the flows between them:

- **Nutrient flows:** Where does fertility come from? (Animal manure → compost → garden. Kitchen scraps → chickens or compost. Cover crop biomass → soil.)
- **Food flows:** What produces food? Where does it go? (Garden → kitchen. Orchard → preservation. Animals → eggs → kitchen, meat → freezer.)
- **Water flows:** Where does water come from and where does it go? (Rain → roof → rain barrel → garden. Rain → pasture → infiltration → groundwater.)
- **Waste flows:** What leaves the system? (Anything that leaves the property — sold products, waste sent to landfill, nutrients in runoff — is a leak in the system.)

Identify gaps: where are you purchasing inputs that could be produced internally? Where are you discarding waste that could be recycled? This analysis is the foundation of the integrated system design at Standing Ground.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Water Bath Canning
**Developing well:** The student follows tested recipes exactly, maintains proper headspace, achieves consistent seals, and can explain why pH determines whether water bath canning is safe.

**Developing slowly:** The student improvises recipes, is sloppy with headspace or rim cleaning, or cannot explain the safety principles.

**Diagnostic questions:** Ask: "Why can you water-bath can pickles but not green beans?" If the student cannot explain the pH/botulism connection, the safety education is incomplete. Do not allow independent canning until this understanding is solid.

### 2. Pressure Canning
**Developing well:** The student operates the pressure canner safely, maintains correct pressure for the full processing time, adjusts for altitude, and understands why pressure canning is required for low-acid foods. They do not take shortcuts.

**Developing slowly:** The student is anxious about the pressure canner (common) and avoids it, or is overconfident and does not maintain proper attention to pressure during processing.

**Diagnostic questions:** Watch the student during pressure canning. Do they monitor the gauge consistently? Do they adjust heat to maintain stable pressure? Do they let pressure return to zero naturally before opening? Each of these is a specific safety checkpoint.

### 3. Fermentation at Scale
**Developing well:** The student produces large batches (5-gallon crock or equivalent) and manages fermentation through to completion. They can troubleshoot common problems (kahm yeast, soft texture, uneven fermentation).

**Developing slowly:** The student makes small jars but has not scaled up. Fermentation failures discourage them.

**Diagnostic questions:** If batches are failing, review the basics: salt ratio, submersion, temperature. Fermentation failures almost always trace to one of these three variables.

### 4. Harvest Planning
**Developing well:** The student plans production quantities based on preservation targets. They can calculate how many plants are needed to produce a year's supply of specific preserved products.

**Developing slowly:** Preservation is reactive (dealing with whatever the garden produces) rather than planned (growing specific quantities for specific preservation goals).

**Diagnostic questions:** Ask: "How many quarts of tomato sauce does your family eat per year? How many tomato plants do you need?" If the student has not connected production planning to preservation planning, this connection needs to be made explicit.

### 5. System Mapping
**Developing well:** The system diagram shows all major enterprises, the flows between them, and identifies at least one gap or leak. The student can explain every connection and propose improvements.

**Developing slowly:** The diagram is incomplete or shows enterprises without connections. The student cannot identify gaps.

**Diagnostic questions:** Walk the property with the student and their diagram. Point to each enterprise: "What goes in? What comes out? Where does the output go?" If the student cannot trace the flows, the diagram needs more observation-based work.
